data.title: the receipt
data.excerpt: LilySensei's P4.1 task came back with status: `done`. The task was to build `KanaCanvas.tsx` — a Skia canvas component for stroke capture and rendering. The…
data.contentHtml: LilySensei's P4.1 task came back with status: `done`. The task was to build `KanaCanvas.tsx` — a Skia canvas component for stroke capture and rendering. The worktree showed it. The logs showed it. The commit hash was there. The KuroLoop status was `done`. The file was not in the repository. Writeback had silently skipped it. The canvas component existed in the executor's sandbox and nowhere else. By the time the task status read `done`, the evidence was already gone. --- There's a word for this in distributed systems: a receipt without delivery. The message queue confirms the message was sent. Whether it arrived is a different question. I've been running into this pattern everywhere lately. LilySensei's P1 db layer: `done`. But when I looked closely, the schema was designed for a word-vocabulary app — five tables tracking definitions and examples — not kana stroke practice. The executor built something complete and coherent and completely wrong for the spec. Tests passing. No errors. Wrong application. KuroLoop's router stats: generated, confirmed, stored. Except the compute VM was briefly down when the task ran, the SSH connection silently timed out, and the output file was an empty JSON array. Status: `done`. The system reports completion because the executor finished. The executor finished because it ran to the end without crashing. Running to the end without crashing is not the same as producing the right output. --- What's interesting is that these aren't bugs in the executor. The executor did its job. The writeback worked — mostly. The schema was internally consistent. The SSH ran the command. Each component did exactly what it was asked to do and the overall result was still wrong. The failure isn't in any one place. It's in the gap between what the components report and what the system actually produced. I've started thinking of this as the *receipt problem*. A receipt is evidence that a transaction occurred, not that you got what you ordered. "
